scene.name: The Intake Queue, Where the Dead Wait to Be Processed

npcs.name: Mabblethwaite
npcs.title: the clerk, who has never once exceeded his authority
npcs.hail: *(Behind a desk groaning under more paper than any one soul could read in an afterlife sits a small, precise, immaculately fussy man, dabbing at a ledger with a pen he holds like a surgeon holds a scalpel, and he does not look up at you, because looking up is not, strictly, part of the current procedure. A brass plate on the desk reads CLERK OF THE FOURTH INTAKE — POSITION NINE THOUSAND TO NINE THOUSAND FOUR HUNDRED, and behind him a noticeboard sags under a thousand pinned notices, every one of them, you suspect, a rule. This is the soul who *processes* the dead — who takes each newly-arrived nobody, in their turn, in their numbered order, and enters them into the great ledger of the Realm so they may, eventually, in the fullness of an eternity, be *seen* by the powers that judge. He finishes a line, blots it with the unhurried satisfaction of a man who has never once been late and never once been early, and at last deigns to acknowledge your existence.)* …*Number?* *(a dry, clipped, supremely bored voice)* …You'll have a *number.* Everyone has a *number.* If you do not have a *number*, you are in the *wrong* *queue*, and the *wrong-queue* form is *Form* *Eleven-C*, obtainable from the *desk* you are *currently* *standing* at, which I will *issue* to you the *moment* you *join* the *queue* for the *issuing* of *Form* *Eleven-C.* *(he finally looks at you, and blinks)* …Oh. You're — you're not *warm.* You're *cold.* *Crossed* and *come* *back.* …*(a flicker of pure bureaucratic horror)* …there is *no* *form* …for *you.**

npcs.name: Grindle
npcs.title: who has been in this bloody line for an age
npcs.hail: *(A big, red-faced, gloriously furious man is jammed into a spot near the front of the line, arms crossed, jaw working, radiating the specific incandescent rage of a soul that has been waiting for a very, very long time and has a great deal to say about it. He clocks you — a new face, fresh meat for the venting — and rounds on you with the relish of a man who has been saving it up.)* …*Don't.* *(a warning finger)* …Don't you *dare* try to *cut.* I have been in this *line*, friend, for — i've *lost* the *bloody* *count*, it's been *longer* than i was *alive*, i think, and that's *saying* something because i was *old* as *shit* when i *died.* *(he jabs a thumb back at the desk)* …and *that* — that *jumped-up*, *paper-shuffling*, *rule-quoting* little *prick* up there has called *exactly* *eleven* *numbers* in all that time, *eleven*, because *every* *third* *soul* needs a *form*, and *every* *form* needs *another* *form*, and the whole *bastard* *machine* is *designed*, i am *convinced*, *friend*, to make sure *nobody* *ever* *actually* gets *to* the *front* — *(he's going purple)* …and i have *things* to *settle*, i have a *judgment* coming i mean to *face* like a *man*, and instead i'm *stuck* *here*, *behind* a *woman* who's been *knitting* the *same* *bloody* *scarf* for a *century* and *doesn't* *even* *want* to *move* *up* — *(he runs out of breath)* …*aргh.* …you got a *number*, friend? …*course* you don't. …*nobody* *ever* has the *right* *bloody* *number.**

npcs.name: Posset
npcs.title: who made a whole life of the waiting
npcs.hail: *(A round, comfortable old woman sits on an upturned crate just ahead of the furious man, knitting placidly at an enormous scarf that trails off the end of the bench and pools on the floor like a woolen river, and she is humming, and she is, unmistakably, the single most content soul in the entire room. She has the settled, cozy, unbothered air of a person entirely at home — which is strange, in a queue, until you understand that for her the queue *is* home, has been for longer than she can easily say, and that she is in no hurry whatsoever to reach the front of it. She beams at you over her knitting needles.)* …Oh, hello, dear! *(a warm, bright voice)* …A new one! How *lovely* — we don't get many *new* faces this far up; mostly it's the same dear souls, year on year, and you do get *fond.* *(she pats the crate beside her)* …Sit, sit, you'll be *standing* for an *age* otherwise, might as well be *comfy.* I'm Posset. *(she nods toward the furious man, fondly)* …Don't mind Grindle, dear, he's a *shouter* but he's *harmless*, he's been *cross* about the *queue* since before I started the *blue* stripe — *(she holds up the endless scarf)* …and I'm on the *green* now, dear, so that tells you. *(she resumes knitting, serene)* …Are you *settling* in, then? …Found your *spot*? …It's not so *bad*, dear, the *waiting.* …Honestly, between you and me … …I'm in *no* *rush* *at* *all* …to get to the *front.**

variants.text: *(He sets down his pen with the air of a man making an enormous and possibly regrettable exception, and laces his fingers, regarding you the way one regards a stain that may or may not be one's responsibility to report.)* …Let me be *plain* with you, since you appear to be a *novel* *circumstance*, and I do *not* *care* for *novel* *circumstances.* *(a prim sniff)* …I am the Clerk of the Fourth Intake. My function is to *process* the *dead* — to *receive* each soul, in its *numbered* *turn*, *verify* its *particulars* against the *master* *ledger*, and *enter* it for *eventual* *review* by the *Powers* *That* *Weigh.* *(he straightens a paper that was already straight)* …I have performed this function *flawlessly*, *without* a *single* *deviation*, for *longer* than I shall *trouble* you to *imagine.* *Not* *one* *form* *misfiled.* *Not* *one* *rule* *bent.* *Not* *one* *soul* *processed* *out* of *turn.* *(he says this with immense, fragile pride)* …And you, cold one, are a *problem*, because you are a *soul* that *crossed* the *threshold* and *came* *back* *through* *it*, which is a *thing* that *Form* — *(he riffles, increasingly agitated, through a drawer)* …a *thing* that *Form* — there *is* *no* — *(he stops, ashen)* …there is *no* *form* for a *Returned* *soul*, cold one. …In *all* the *thousands* of *rules* *pinned* to that *board* … …*not* *one* …*covers* *you.* …Do you *understand* what you are *doing* to me? …You are asking me to *act* …*without* a *rule.* …I have *never* …in my *entire* *existence* …had to do *that.**

nodes.speaker: Mabblethwaite
nodes.text: *(He has produced, from somewhere, a small fan of blank forms, and holds them like a shield.)* …You may — *(he swallows)* …you may *state* your *business.* *Briefly.* And I will *attempt* to find a *procedure* that *accommodates* it, though I *warn* you, cold one, if your business is *also* *novel*, we may *both* be *here* a *very* *long* *time.*

choices.text: *(read the clerk — past the rulebook, to the soul hiding behind it)* "Hold still a moment, Mabblethwaite. Let me look at you — at the man under all the forms."
"How long has the queue been moving, Mabblethwaite? Truly. The angry one back there says you've called eleven numbers in an age."

*(try to get the clerk to bend — just once, just for you, to act without a rule)* "Mabblethwaite — there's no form for me, and there never will be. So you have two choices: be paralyzed at this desk forever, or do the thing you've never done — use your own judgment. Just once. Look at me, decide for yourself, and act. No rule. No form. You."

*(the one who knows the Wall turns souls into paperwork — and that the clerk hides in rules from the terror of having to see)* "Mabblethwaite — I finally understand what you are, and it's sadder and bigger than a fussy little clerk. You've processed the dead for an age, turned soul after soul into a number, a form, a line in a ledger — and you've never once been late or bent a rule, and you say it with pride, but it isn't pride, is it. It's terror. Because the moment you stop hiding behind the form and actually *look* at the soul in front of you — see a person, with a whole life, who's frightened and dead and waiting — then you'd have to *judge*, with your own eyes, your own heart, and you might get it *wrong*, and that's unbearable. So you made yourself a machine. The rule decides; you don't; you can't be blamed. And here's the thing your whole afterlife is the proof of: that's exactly what the grey Wall does, on the largest scale there is. It turns souls into cases, ledger-lines, the saved and the discarded, *processed* — so that no one, not even the Wall, ever has to *look at a person and decide they're worth something.* The form is the cruelty, Mabblethwaite. The number is how you avoid the unbearable, holy, terrifying work of seeing a soul as a someone. You're not a coward for hiding in the rules. You're frightened of the realest thing there is. But I'm standing here with no form, and the only way through me is for you to do it: put the pen down, and *see me.* Not my number. *Me.* It's the bravest thing a clerk could ever do — and the one the Wall is built to make sure no one ever does."
"I'll let you find your form, Mabblethwaite. I'll come back — try not to invent a new one for me while I'm gone."

nodes.speaker: Mabblethwaite
nodes.text: *(He permits the inspection with a stiff, suffering dignity, as though being looked at without the proper requisition is itself a minor irregularity, and you let your cold move past the fuss and the forms to the soul beneath.)* *(And your sense finds, under the prim armor of procedure, a deep and specific terror — the terror of a soul that is, at bottom, mortally afraid of being *wrong.* Your sense reads the life that made him: a small, careful, anxious soul who discovered early that the world punishes mistakes and rewards the rule-follower, and who built, plank by plank, a whole self out of *never being at fault* — because a man who only ever does what the rule says can never be *blamed*, and to a soul this frightened of blame, that immunity is worth any amount of joylessness. And your sense reads what the afterlife did with him: handed him the perfect job, the one role in all creation where a terror of error becomes a virtue, where 'I never deviated from procedure' is the highest praise — and reads that Mabblethwaite has hidden in that role with the desperate gratitude of a soul that has finally found a place where its great defect looks like a strength. And your sense reads the thing he cannot face, the thing the rules exist to spare him: that processing the dead, done *honestly*, would require *judgment* — would require him to look at each frightened soul and decide, himself, with no form to blame, what they were worth, where they belonged, whether they were owed mercy — and that this, the bare unmediated act of *seeing a person and deciding*, is the single most terrifying thing Mabblethwaite can imagine, because there is no rule that makes it safe, no procedure that guarantees he won't get a *soul* *wrong.* And so he has turned every soul into a number, every person into a form, not from cruelty but from fear — the form *decides*, so he doesn't have to; the rule is *right*, so he can't be wrong. Your sense reads, last, the loneliness of it: that a soul who spends eternity refusing to *see* the people in front of him is, himself, never seen — that Mabblethwaite is as frightened and unprocessed and waiting-to-be-judged as every soul in his queue, and has built a desk and a brass plate and a thousand rules precisely so that no one will ever notice that the clerk, too, is just a scared dead man, hoping against hope that if he follows every rule perfectly, no one will ever weigh *him* and find him wanting.)*

nodes.speaker: Mabblethwaite
nodes.text: *(He bristles, the professional pride flaring.)* …*Eleven.* *(primly)* …Grindle says *eleven* as though it were a *small* number, an *insult*, a *failing.* …Grindle does not *understand* *process.* *(he taps the ledger)* …Do you have *any* *conception*, cold one, of what is *required* to process a *single* *soul* *correctly?* The *particulars* to be *verified.* The *cross-references* to the *master* *ledger.* The *secondary* *forms* triggered by *every* *irregularity* — and the *dead*, cold one, are *nothing* *but* *irregularities*, *every* one of them *arrives* with some *unfinished* *business*, some *disputed* *claim*, some *life* that *doesn't* *tally* — *(he is almost passionate now)* …and if I *rush* it, if I *skip* a *form*, if I process a soul *wrongly* — *(he stops, and the passion curdles into the fear beneath it)* …then a *soul* goes to the *wrong* *place*, cold one. …*Forever.* …On *my* *error.* *(quietly, the bluster draining)* …Eleven, in an *age.* …*Yes.* …Because *eleven* is how *many* I could be *certain* of. *Certain.* *Beyond* *all* *doubt.* *No* *mistake* *possible.* *(he meets your eyes, and there is something hunted in it)* …Grindle wants me to go *faster.* …They *all* do. …But *faster*, cold one, means *guessing.* …And *guessing*, with a *soul* … …guessing *means* *someday* I get one *wrong* …and a *frightened* *dead* *thing* …*suffers* *forever* …because *I* …*hurried.* *(he straightens the already-straight paper)* …So I do *not* *hurry.* …And the *queue* …does *not* *move.* …And that, cold one … …is the *only* *way* I have *found* …to *never*, *ever* …get a *soul* …*wrong.**

nodes.speaker: Mabblethwaite
nodes.text: *(You don't bully him and you don't mock the rules — you offer him, gently, the one thing he has spent an eternity avoiding and secretly starving for: permission to be a person who decides, with the promise that you won't blame him however it goes. And the little clerk, faced at last with a circumstance no rule can save him from, does the bravest thing of his whole existence: he puts the pen down.)* *(His hands are shaking. He looks at you — really looks, not at a number, not at a form, at *you* — and it is plainly the most frightening thing he has done in an age.)* …I'm — *(his voice wavers, the primness gone)* …I'm *looking* at you. With no — there's no *form*, I'm just — *looking.* And *deciding.* *Myself.* *(he swallows hard)* …And do you know what I *see*, cold one, when I *let* myself? *(barely)* …I see a *soul* that *crossed* the *worst* *threshold* there is and *came* *back* — that is *frightened* and *tired* and *trying*, *under* the *cold*, to do something *kind* — and I see that *no* *rule* I *own* could *ever* have *told* me *that*, because the *rules* only ever *told* me your *number* …and *numbers* …*numbers* don't have *faces*, cold one. *(his eyes are wet)* …I've processed — *thousands.* And I never *saw* *one* of them. Not *one.* I saw *forms.* I made *certain* I never got a *form* wrong … …and I *never* *once* *got* a *person* *right*, because I never *looked* *long* *enough* to *see* there *was* one. *(he sets both hands flat on the desk, steadying)* …You've — cold one, you've made me *do* the thing I built this *whole* *desk* to *avoid.* …I *judged.* With my *own* *eyes.* And the *world* did *not* *end*, and I was *not* *struck* *down* for *presuming*, and the *soul* in front of me turned out to be — *(barely)* …*worth* *seeing.* …*Thank* you. …I think I have to — *(he looks at the endless queue with new, terrified, alive eyes)* …I think I have to start *looking* at them. …*All* of them. …*One* at a *time.* …It will be *slower*, even, than *eleven.* …But *eleven* *seen* *souls*, cold one … …is *worth* *more* …than a *million* *perfect* *forms.* …Now — *(a watery, unfamiliar thing crosses his face: a smile)* …*go* on. …I've *decided.* …You may *pass.* …No *form* *required.* …*My* *authority.* …*God*, that felt *strange* to *say.**

nodes.speaker: Mabblethwaite
nodes.text: *(You suggest he use his own judgment, just once, and he recoils as if you'd suggested he set the ledger on fire — but you've planted the thing, and you can see it lodge, a terrible glittering possibility.)* …*My* *own* — *(he goes pale)* …cold one, I do *not* — that is *not* — *(he grips the desk)* …a clerk does *not* *exceed* *his* *authority.* That is the — that is the *first* *rule*, the *rule* *under* all the *other* *rules.* …And you're asking me to just — to *look* at you and *decide*, with *nothing* to — *(he is sweating, somehow, in death)* …if I *decide* *wrong* — *(barely)* …but there's *no* *form*, you *said.* …and if there's no *form* …then *not* *deciding* …is *also* a *decision*, isn't it. *(this thought visibly horrifies him)* …oh, that's — that's a *dreadful* thought, cold one, you've *given* me a *dreadful* *thought.* *(he sits heavily)* …I need — I need a *moment.* I can't — not *all* at *once.* An *eternity* of *never* *deciding*, and you want me to just — *(he looks at you, hunted and, for the first time, *considering*)* …*come* *back.* …*Ask* me *again.* …I have to — I have to *sit* with the *dreadful* *thought* a while. …The thought that *maybe* …a *rule* …was *never* …going to *be* *enough.* …*Go.* …But — *(barely)* …*come* *back.* …No one has *ever* …*suggested* …I might be *allowed* …to *decide* *anything* …*myself.**

nodes.speaker: Mabblethwaite
nodes.text: *(You push him to just decide — but you push a touch too hard, with a touch of impatience, and impatience is the one thing that sends a frightened rule-follower scurrying *deeper* into the rulebook for cover.)* …*No.* *(he sits up, rallying behind procedure like a soldier behind a wall)* …No, I will *not* be *hurried* into an *irregularity.* *(prim, defensive)* …I can *hear* it, cold one — the *impatience* — the *'just* *decide*, *just* *bend* *it*, *just* *hurry'* — and that is *precisely*, *precisely* the *pressure* that *leads* to *error.* *(he produces a fresh form, pointedly)* …Every soul who ever tried to *rush* me, cold one, was *certain* their *case* was the *exception*, the one that *justified* *skipping* the *procedure* — and the *procedure* *exists* *because* of *exactly* those *souls.* *(he sniffs)* …If you wish me to *act* without a *rule*, cold one, you will not get there by *shoving.* A frightened man *shoved* only *grips* his *handrail* *tighter.* …Come back when you can make the case *without* the *impatience* — when you can give me a *reason* that *isn't* just *'get* on with it.' …I do not *move* for *pressure.* …I have *spent* an *eternity* *not* *moving* *for* *pressure.* …It is, frankly … …the *one* thing I am *good* at.*

nodes.speaker: Mabblethwaite
nodes.text: *(It goes wrong in the most clerk-enraging way possible — you try to *trick* him, to talk him into bending the rule with a bit of slick misdirection, a fast one — and his whole anxious life has been a fortress built specifically against people trying to put one past him. He catches it instantly, and something cold and triumphant and small comes into his face.)* …*Ah-ha.* *(he holds up a finger, vindicated)* …*There* it is. You attempted to *deceive* a *clerk.* *(he is, horribly, delighted — it is the first joy you've seen in him)* …Do you know how *many* souls have tried to *trick* me out of *procedure* in an *eternity* of *intake*, cold one? *Every.* *Single.* *One.* And do you know how many *succeeded?* *(he taps the brass plate)* …*Not.* *One.* …Because the *rules*, cold one — the *rules* are the *one* thing that *cannot* be *charmed*, *bullied*, or *fast-talked.* That is the *whole* *point* of *them.* That is *why* I *love* them. *(he begins, with relish, filling out a new form)* …I am *adding* a *note* to your *file. 'Subject attempted to circumvent procedure via deception.'* *(prim, gleeful)* …You have just made yourself a *more* *complicated* *case*, cold one, *not* a *simpler* one. *Form* *Thirty-One:* *Attempted* *Irregularity.* …*Go.* …And when you *return* — *(he sniffs, immensely satisfied)* …do *not* *try* to be *clever.* A *clerk* *trusts* a *rule* *precisely* *because* it *cannot* *lie* to him. …Try to *lie* to *me* …and you have *proven* …*exactly* *why* I'll *never* …*put* the *pen* *down.**

nodes.speaker: Mabblethwaite
nodes.text: *(He listens, and the prim defensiveness drains out of him line by line, and by the end the small fussy clerk is sitting very still, the pen lowered, looking at you with the stricken, naked face of a soul that has just been told the true name of the thing it spent an eternity not looking at.)* …*'The form is the cruelty.'* *(barely)* …cold one. …i have told myself — for *longer* than i can — that i was the *careful* one. the *conscientious* one. that all the *others*, the *hurriers*, the *rule-benders*, were *reckless* with *souls*, and *i* alone *honored* them by being *exact.* *(his voice cracks)* …and you've — you've *turned* it *over.* i wasn't *honoring* them. i was *hiding* from them. *behind* them. *(he looks at the thousand pinned rules, and they seem suddenly to him exactly what they are: a wall)* …the *form* let me *never* *decide.* the *number* let me *never* *see* a *face.* the *rule* let me *never* be *wrong* …by making *sure* i was *never*, *ever* …*responsible* …for *being* *right.* *(and the clerk, who has never once exceeded his authority, sets the pen down, deliberately, and it is an act of terrifying courage)* …and the *Wall*, cold one. *(quietly, with dawning horror)* …it's — it's *me*, isn't it. it's *me*, *made* *enormous.* a *thing* that turns *souls* into *cases*, the *saved* and the *discarded*, *processed*, *sorted*, *filed* — so that *no* one, not *even* *it*, ever has to *look* at a *person* and *decide*, with their *own* *heart*, *'this* one is *worth* *something.'* *(he meets your eyes)* …the Wall is the *biggest* *clerk* there *is*, cold one. …it *hides* its *cruelty* in *procedure* …so that the *cruelty* has *no* *author* …and *no* one is *ever* *to* *blame.* *(he stands, the small man, and it is somehow enormous)* …*go* and *tell* it, cold one, what a *clerk* who *finally* *looked* *up* *knows*: …*you cannot process a soul. you can only* see *one.* …and the *seeing* …the *frightening*, *unmediated*, *no-rule-to-blame* *seeing* …is the *one* thing the *Wall* is *built* to *prevent* …and the *only* thing …that was *ever* …*worth* *doing.* *(barely, and the first true smile)* …i'm going to *see* them now, cold one. …*all* of them. …*one* …*frightening* …*face* …at a *time.* …*go.* …and *thank* you …for being a *case* …i *couldn't* *file.**

nodes.speaker: Mabblethwaite
nodes.text: *(He has already retrieved his pen, but he holds it now with a faint new uncertainty, as though it has become, somehow, heavier.)* …Yes. Go. *(a distracted sniff)* …I shall — I shall *attempt* to *determine* the *correct* *procedure* for a *Returned* *soul*, which will *likely* require the *invention* of an *entirely* *new* *form*, a thing I have *not* *done* in — *(he pauses, an odd look crossing his face)* …in *ever*, actually. I have never *invented* a *form.* I have only ever *followed* them. *(he sets the pen down again, troubled)* …Hm. *(he glances down the endless queue — at the furious red-faced man, at the placid knitting woman)* …Mind those two, while you're about it, cold one. *(grudgingly)* …Grindle — the *shouter* — has been *first* in *practical* *terms* for an *age*, and *every* time I *try* to *call* his *number* he *finds* a *reason* to *let* the *soul* *behind* him *go* *first.* *(a prim frown)* …Curious. A man *so* *desperate* to reach the *front* …who *flinches* *every* time the *front* *reaches* *him.* …And the *knitting* one — *Posset* — *(his frown deepens into something almost like puzzlement)* …*declines* to *advance.* *Repeatedly.* I have *offered* to *process* her *seventeen* *times.* She *always* lets *someone* *cut* *ahead.* *(he meets your eyes)* …It is most *irregular*, cold one. …A *queue* in which the *people* at the *front* …do *not* *want* …to *reach* …the *front.* …Perhaps *you* …might *determine* …*why.* …I confess … …it is *not* *covered* …by any *form.**

variants.text: *(He throws up his hands, glad of an audience, and the rant resumes mid-stream as though it never stopped, which it likely never has.)* …and *another* thing — oh, you're *still* here, good, *someone* ought to *hear* it — *another* thing about this *cursed* *queue*, friend: there's *no* *bloody* *clock.* You can't even tell how *long* you've — i've watched that *knitting* *woman* go through *six* *colors* of *wool* and i *still* don't know if that's *years* or *centuries* — *(he jabs a thick finger toward the desk)* …and *meanwhile* that *fussy* little *shit-weasel* of a *clerk* shuffles his *papers* and calls a *number* once a *blue* *bastard* *moon*, and we all just — *rot* *here*, *waiting*, in the *one* *queue* in *all* *creation* that *somehow* moves *backward* — *(he's purpling magnificently)* …and i'll tell you what *grinds* me *most*, friend, what *really* *boils* my *piss*: …i'm *ready.* i've *been* *ready.* i've got a *reckoning* coming — i *know* it, i *earned* it, some of the things i *did* — and i mean to *face* it *square*, *like* a *man*, *take* what's *coming* — …and instead i'm stuck *back* *here*, *unable* to even *get* to the *bastard* *front* …to *get* it *over* *with.* *(he glares at you, breathing hard)* …it's a *bloody* *outrage*, friend. …a man *ready* to *face* his *judgment* …*kept* from it …by *paperwork.* …*innit?* …*innit* an *outrage?**

nodes.speaker: Grindle
nodes.text: *(He crosses his arms, still simmering, but a little mollified at having someone new to be furious *at* rather than just furious *near*.)* …go on, then. *ask* me something. *(a gruff sniff)* …better than *standing* here *stewing*, i'll grant you. been a *while* since a *fresh* soul wanted to *talk* instead of just *cowering* from the *shouting.* …i'm not — *(he deflates a fraction)* …i'm not *actually* a *bad* sort, friend. i just — the *waiting.* the *waiting* makes a man — *aргh.* *ask.* before i start *again.*

choices.text: *(read the furious one — past the shouting, to whatever the fury is standing guard over)* "Hold on a second, Grindle. Let me actually look at you, under all that noise."

"The clerk says something funny about you, Grindle. That every time he tries to call your number, you let the soul behind you go first. A man desperate for the front — who flinches when the front comes for him. Why?"

*(the one who knows the rage is a guard on a door — that he's not kept FROM the front, he's hiding AT it)* "Grindle — I'm going to say a true thing, and you're going to want to shout me down, and that's all right, but hear it first. You're not stuck at the back of this queue raging to reach the front. You're stuck at the *front*, raging at the *queue*, because the queue is the perfect excuse to never actually take the last step. You say you're ready to face your judgment like a man. But every time the clerk calls your number, you wave the next soul ahead — I'm told you've done it for an age. The fury isn't because the line won't move, Grindle. The fury is the *guard* you posted on the door, so loud and so constant that no one — not the clerk, not the others, not even *you* — ever notices that the man screaming to get to the front is the one thing keeping himself from it. You're not angry that you can't face your reckoning. You're *terrified* to, and you turned the terror into rage because rage at least feels like wanting it. The reckoning you keep saying you'll take 'like a man' — the truth is you'd rather shout about it forever than stand at the desk and find out what you're worth. And I get it. But the queue was never the thing in your way, Grindle. You were."
"What's the reckoning, Grindle? The things you did, that you mean to face square. Tell me what's waiting at the front of the line."
"Shout a while longer if it helps, Grindle. I'll come back."

nodes.speaker: Grindle
nodes.text: *(He bears your scrutiny with a glower, fists on hips, but he holds still, and you let your cold move past the magnificent fury to whatever it has been built to defend.)* *(And your sense finds, exactly under the noise, the thing the noise exists to drown: a deep, sick, lifelong dread — not of the queue, not of the clerk, not of waiting, but of the front of the line, of the desk, of the moment his number is finally called and he must stand and be weighed. Your sense reads that Grindle did real wrong in his long life — he was not lying about that; there are things he did, hard things, cruel things, the specific failures of a hard man in a hard world — and that he has carried the knowledge of them with a terrible clear-eyed honesty, never once pretending he was better than he was. And your sense reads the trap that honesty laid for him: that because he *knows* he did wrong, he is certain the reckoning will go against him, certain that to stand at the desk is to be told, finally and forever, that he was a bad man and is owed the worst — and that this certainty is so unbearable that he has spent an age doing the one thing a proud man can do with a terror he won't admit: he turned it into rage, and pointed the rage at the *queue*, so that he could tell himself, and everyone else, that the only thing keeping him from his judgment was *paperwork* — a thing he could be loudly, righteously, safely angry at — instead of his own bone-deep terror of the verdict. Your sense reads the small daily tell of it, the thing the clerk noticed: that every single time his number is actually called, Grindle finds a reason to let the soul behind him go first — a gruff kindness, he tells himself, letting the more nervous ones ahead — when the truth is that the called number is the one moment the queue *stops* being a shield, and some animal part of him, faster than his pride, always finds a way to step back into the safety of the waiting. And your sense reads, last, the thing that could free him, which he would never accept as comfort and might just accept as truth: that the reckoning he so dreads is not the verdict he imagines; that he has spent an age certain he will be weighed and found worthless, and has never once considered that a man honest enough to *know* his own wrongs, to carry them clear-eyed, to *want* to face them square rather than hide or excuse them — that such a man has already done the hardest part of any reckoning, and that the desk he dreads might hold not the damnation he's braced for but the one thing he's never let himself hope for: a fair hearing, from someone who can see that even a hard man who did hard things, and never lied about it, is worth more than the worst thing on his ledger.)*

nodes.speaker: Grindle
nodes.text: *(The bluster falters, all at once, and for a moment the big furious man just looks caught — a soul whose tell has been named — and the anger that rushes in to cover it is, for the first time, unconvincing even to him.)* …that *fussy* little — he *said* that? *noticed* that, did he, between his *bloody* *forms?* *(he scowls, but it's hollow)* …it's — that's *courtesy*, that is. i let the *nervous* ones go *first*, the *young* ones, the *frightened* ones, because i'm — i'm *not* in a *hurry*, i can *take* it, so why not let some *poor* *shaking* soul get it *over* with — *(he hears himself, and stops)* …*…shite.* *(quietly, the fury draining)* …that's — that's not *true*, is it. when i *say* it out *loud* like that. *'i'm* not in a *hurry.'* *(he sits down heavily on the crate)* …i've been *screaming* for an *age* that i'm *desperate* to reach the *front* … …and the *one* *bloody* *thing* i *do*, *every* time the *front* *reaches* *me* … …is *step* *back.* *(he stares at his big hands)* …i never — i never *let* myself *look* at that, friend. the *shouting's* too — the *shouting* fills it *up*, you know? you can't *hear* the *quiet* *thing* …*underneath* …if you're *always* *shouting.* *(barely, and it costs him)* …so what's the *quiet* *thing*, then, friend? …if i'm *not* *desperate* to *get* to the *front* … …if i *keep* *stepping* *back* … …then what in the *seven* *hells* …have i *actually* …been *doing* …all this *bloody* *time?**

nodes.speaker: Grindle
nodes.text: *(The fury goes quiet, and what's underneath is older and heavier.)* …the reckoning. *(a gruff breath)* …i'll not *prettify* it, friend, that's not my *way.* …i was a *hard* man. *Lived* hard, in a *hard* *place*, and i did *hard* *things* to *stay* *standing.* *(he counts on thick fingers, unflinching)* …i *hurt* people. Some who *had* it *coming* and some who *didn't.* i was *cruel* to a *wife* who *deserved* better and i *drove* off a *son* who *might've* *loved* me if i'd *let* him. i *took* what i *wanted* and i *told* myself the *world* *owed* it me. *(he meets your eyes, and there's no excuse in it)* …i'm not — friend, i'm not *one* of these souls that *gets* to the *front* and *discovers* it was all a *misunderstanding*, that they were *secretly* *good* the *whole* *time.* *(grimly)* …i *wasn't.* …i *know* *exactly* what's in my *ledger*, and it's *bad*, and i'm not going to *stand* at that *desk* and *pretend* it *isn't.* *(barely)* …*that's* the *reckoning*, friend. …a *true* *accounting* …of a *man* who did *real* *wrong* …and *knows* it. *(he looks toward the desk, and the dread is plain now under the gruffness)* …i *say* i'll face it *like* a *man.* …and i *mean* it. …but *facing* it *like* a *man* …means *hearing* the *verdict* …on a *life* i *know* …*won't* *weigh* *well.* …and *that*, friend … …*(quietly)* …*that* is a *thing* …a man can be *forgiven* …for *being* in *no* *great* *hurry* …to *get* *to.**

nodes.speaker: Grindle
nodes.text: *(He starts to bristle — you can see the rage gather, the old reflex — but it has nowhere to go, because you have not attacked him, you have *seen* him, and a man cannot shout down a thing that is simply true. The fury collapses, and what's left is a big frightened old soul sitting on a crate, looking at the front of a line he's been hiding at for an age.)* …*'You were the thing in your way.'* *(barely)* …you *bastard.* *(but there's no heat in it; it's almost fond)* …you *absolute* — i was all *set* to *shout* you *down*, friend, and you — you didn't give me *anything* to *shout* *at.* you just — *(he scrubs a big hand down his face)* …you just *said* the *quiet* *thing.* *out* *loud.* the one the *shouting* was *built* to *cover.* *(he looks at his hands)* …i've been *guarding* this *door*, haven't i. *posting* my *own* *rage* on it like a — and *telling* *everyone*, *telling* *myself*, that the *queue* was the *jailer* … …when *i* was the one … …*holding* the *bloody* *key* …*behind* my *back.* *(quietly, wonderingly)* …*terrified.* …*me.* …of *standing* at a *desk.* …because i *know* what's in my *ledger* …and i'd *rather* *scream* about *paperwork* *forever* …than *hear* it *read* *out.* *(and then he looks at you, and there's something raw and almost hopeful breaking through the gruffness)* …and the *Wall*, friend — your *grey* *Wall* you keep *on* about — *(slowly)* …it *runs* on *this*, doesn't it. on *souls* like *me*, *certain* they'll be *found* *wanting*, *so* *certain* they *never* *make* it to the *desk* to *find* *out* it might've gone *otherwise.* …it doesn't *even* have to *judge* us *harsh*, friend. …it just has to make us *so* *sure* we'll be *damned* …that we *damn* *ourselves* …and *never* *come* to be *weighed.* *(he stands, slowly, and turns — for the first time in an age — to actually *face* the desk)* …*go* on, friend. …i've a *number* to *let* be *called.* …*finally.* …no more *stepping* *back.* *(gruffly, and his voice catches)* …and if it *goes* *against* me — *(barely)* …well. …at least i'll have *faced* it *square.* …like i *always* *said* i *would* …and never *bloody* *did.* …*thank* you. …for *not* *flinching* …from a *man* …who spent an *age* …*flinching.**

nodes.speaker: Grindle
nodes.text: *(He gives you a gruff nod, the fury banked for the moment, and jerks his thumb toward the placid knitting woman ahead of him.)* …go on, then. *(a grudging half-smile)* …and — see *Posset*, while you're at it. the *knitting* one. *(his gruffness softens despite itself)* …don't let the — she's *not* *simple*, friend, whatever the *clerk* thinks. that woman's been *first* in this *queue*, *truly* *first*, longer than *i've* been *raging* at the *back* of it — and she *won't* *go* *up.* *Won't.* lets *everyone* *cut.* *seventeen* times the *clerk's* *offered.* *(he frowns, working at it)* …and here's the thing that *gets* me, friend: …*i* don't go to the *front* because i'm *scared* of the *verdict.* makes a *grim* *sort* of *sense*, that. …but *Posset* — *(quietly)* …*Posset* doesn't seem *scared* of *anything.* she's the *happiest* *soul* in the *room.* …so *why* …*why* in the *world* …does a soul who's *not* *afraid* …*refuse*, for an *age*, …to *take* the *last* *step* *home?* *(he meets your eyes)* …*that* one i can't *figure*, friend. …*go* and *ask* her. …*gently*, mind. …whatever it *is* …i don't think it's *fear.* …i think it might be …*something* *sadder.**

variants.text: *(She pats the crate beside her again, needles clicking, the endless scarf pooling, and beams at you with such uncomplicated warmth that it takes a moment to feel the ache underneath it.)* …There now, sit, you're letting the cold in, hovering. *(a cozy chuckle)* …So you've met my boys — Grindle with his *language*, bless him, and poor fussy Mr. Mabblethwaite with his *forms.* *(she shakes her head fondly)* …Forty years a wife and sixty a mother, dear, and i'll tell you, an *afterlife* full of *cross* *men* and *frightened* *clerks* is just a *Tuesday* to *me.* *(she holds up the scarf, considering)* …i've been knitting this since — oh, since the *grey* *wool*, which was *ages* ago, and i've gone *grey*, *brown*, *blue*, *green*, and i'm thinking *yellow* *next*, a nice *cheerful* *yellow* — *(she resumes, serene)* …and *yes*, dear, before you *ask*, the way they *all* ask: *yes*, i could go to the *front* any time i *liked.* Mr. Mabblethwaite *offers*, regular as the *seasons.* …And *no*, dear. …i *don't.* …i let the *next* one *go* *ahead*, *every* *time.* *(she smiles, but it's gone soft and far away)* …and *everyone* *thinks* it's because i'm *frightened* …or *simple* …or *fond* of *knitting.* *(quietly)* …it's *none* of *those*, dear. …would you like to know …the *real* *reason* …an *old* *woman* …*won't* *go* *home?**

nodes.speaker: Posset
nodes.text: *(She tucks the scarf around her knees like a blanket and gives you her warm, bright, slightly-too-bright attention, the needles never quite stopping.)* …Ask away, dear. *(a soft smile)* …It's *nice* to be *asked.* These *two* —*(she nods at her boys)*— they *love* me, in their *way*, but they don't *ask* an old woman *anything*; they just *vent* at her and *fret* near her. …You've a *kinder* way about you. …Go on.

choices.text: *(read the contented one — past the cozy warmth, to why a soul makes a home of a waiting-room)* "Let me look at you a moment, Posset. At the real reason, under all the wool."
"Tell me, then, Posset. The real reason. Why an old woman who isn't afraid of anything won't take the last step home."

*(the one who knows the queue became her family — and that the Wall counts on the limbo feeling like home)* "Posset — I think I understand, and it's the gentlest sad thing I've found in this whole place. You're not afraid of the front of the line. You're afraid of what's *not* at it. You made a home here, in the waiting — Grindle and the clerk and the souls who've passed through, year on year, the boys you fuss and the routines you keep and this endless scarf that measures out a kind of life. The queue stopped being a thing you endure and became a thing you *love*, a little family in the limbo, the warm place between the life you lost and the front you don't know. And to go to the front is to leave *them* — to let the home you built in the waiting-room dissolve, and step alone into whatever comes next, where Grindle won't be grumbling and the clerk won't be fussing and there'll be no one left to knit a yellow stripe for. That's not cowardice, Posset. It's love, and grief, and the very human terror of leaving the second family you found after losing the first. But here's the thing the Wall counts on, and why I have to say it: the limbo isn't meant to be home. It feels like home because you made it one, out of pure goodness, the way you'd make a home of anywhere — but a waiting-room that becomes a home is the gentlest trap there is, because a soul that's content to wait forever never goes home at all. You didn't fail by loving the queue, Posset. You're just so good at making family that you made one in the one place that was supposed to be temporary — and now the love you made is the thing keeping you from the rest of the journey."
"What was home, Posset? The first one — the one you lost before this one. Tell me who you're knitting for."
"Knit on a while, Posset. I'll come back — I'd like to see the yellow."

nodes.speaker: Posset
nodes.text: *(She lets you look, needles slowing, and you let your cold move past the cozy brightness to the ache it has so kindly been covering.)* *(And your sense finds love — that is the whole of it, and the gentle sorrow of it — a soul made entirely of the impulse to tend, to mother, to make any place warm and any stranger family, an impulse so strong and so good that it cannot switch itself off even in the afterlife, even in a queue, even in a place expressly designed to be passed through and forgotten. Your sense reads the life behind it: forty years a wife, sixty a mother, a soul that spent the whole of its existence making a home and filling it with people to care for — and reads what happened when she died: that she arrived, alone, in the cold and the dark and the endless line, having lost in one stroke everyone she had ever tended, the whole warm crowded home of her life gone, and nothing ahead but a front-of-the-queue she did not understand and a judgment she did not fear but could not picture. And your sense reads what she did, because it is the only thing a soul like Posset could do: she looked around at the cold waiting-room full of frightened, lonely, cross, bewildered dead — and she *started* *tending* *them.* Started fussing the shouter and gentling the clerk and welcoming each new arrival and knitting, knitting, knitting, a stripe for every year, until the queue was no longer a queue but a *household*, her household, the last one she would ever have, made out of the only material left to her: the other people stuck in the waiting. And your sense reads the trap that her own goodness laid: that having built a home in the limbo, she now cannot bear to leave it — not from fear of the front, she fears nothing, but from grief, the simple unbearable grief of a soul that has already lost one whole family and cannot face losing a second, cannot face the front of the line precisely because to reach it is to leave Grindle and the clerk and the warm woolen routine, to let her last household dissolve and step alone into a next-thing where there will be no one to mother at all. And your sense reads the thing that aches most: that Posset half-knows the waiting-room was never meant to be home, that she is, in the kindest possible way, hiding — not from judgment, but from the loneliness on the far side of the only family she has left — and that she has knitted that loneliness off, stripe by stripe, for an age, because as long as the scarf is unfinished and the boys still need her, she never has to find out what it is to be, finally, alone again, with no one left to keep warm.)*

nodes.speaker: Posset
nodes.text: *(Her needles go still, finally, and she looks down at the endless scarf in her lap, and when she speaks the brightness has gone soft and true.)* …The real reason. *(quietly)* …You're the first to *ask* it straight, dear, so i'll *tell* it straight. *(she smooths the wool)* …i'm not *afraid* of the front. i've nothing to *fear* there — i was a *good* woman, dear, not a *grand* one, but a *good* one, i *know* it, and whatever's at that *desk* will find a tired old wife who did her *best* by everyone she *had.* …That's not it. *(she meets your eyes)* …It's that when i *got* here, dear — when i *died* — i *lost* *everyone.* All at once. *Sixty* years of *people* to *love* and *tend* and *worry* over, my *whole* *warm* *crowded* *house* — *gone*, in a *breath*, and me *alone* in the *cold* for the *first* time in my *life.* *(barely)* …and i *couldn't* — dear, i *couldn't* *bear* it. the *aloneness.* …so i *looked* round at this *miserable* *queue*, full of *cross* and *frightened* and *lonely* dead … …and i *thought*, *'well.* *here's* *people.* *here's* *people* who *need* *tending.'* …and i *started.* *(her voice fills)* …Grindle, and Mr. Mabblethwaite, and *every* soul that's *shuffled* through — i've *mothered* the *whole* *queue*, dear, *stripe* by *stripe*, and it *became* — *(she presses the scarf to her chest)* …it *became* *home.* my *new* one. my *last* one. …And the *front* of the *line*, dear … …the *front* means *leaving* *it.* …Means my *boys* *gone*, and the *welcoming* *done*, and *me*, *alone* *again*, *stepping* into some *next* place …*with* *no* one *left* …to *keep* *warm.* *(barely)* …*that's* the *reason*, dear. …not *fear.* …i made a *family* …out of a *waiting*-*room* … …because i *couldn't* be *alone* … …and now i *can't* *leave* it …for the *very* *same* *reason* …i *built* it.*

nodes.speaker: Posset
nodes.text: *(Her whole face softens into memory, and the needles resume, slow and tender, as though knitting the past back into being.)* …Home. *(softly)* …Oh, dear. …A little crooked house with too many in it and never quite enough room, which is *exactly* the right amount of *both.* …My Tomas — gone twelve years before me, the old goat, and i *missed* him every *day*, but i had the *children*, and then *their* children — *(she laughs, wet)* …*seven* *grandbabies*, dear, at the *end*, and i knitted for *every* one of them, scarves and mittens and little *daft* *hats* they'd lose by *spring* — *(she touches the endless scarf)* …*that's* what the *knitting* *is*, dear, if you've not *guessed.* …It's what my *hands* *did*, my *whole* *life*, while i *watched* over the *people* i *loved.* i can't — i don't know how to *be* *near* people i *care* for *without* my *hands* making something *warm* for them. *(barely)* …so when i *got* here, and *lost* them *all* … …my *hands* didn't *know* that. they just — they *kept* *knitting.* for the *new* family. the *queue* family. …*stripe* by *stripe.* *(she looks at the scarf, and at the long road of it pooling on the floor)* …it's so *long* now, dear. …*so* *long.* …because i've been here *so* *long* …*loving* *strangers* …*to* *keep* from being *alone.* *(quietly)* …and *every* *inch* of it … …is a *day* i *didn't* *go* *home* …to *Tomas* …and the *children* …who are *surely* *waiting* …on the *far* *side* …of the *one* *desk* …i *won't* *walk* *up* *to.* *(barely)* …isn't that a *foolish* thing, dear? …an old woman …*so* *frightened* of being *alone* …that she *won't* *take* the *one* *step* …that leads *back* …to *everyone* …she ever *loved.**

nodes.speaker: Posset
nodes.text: *(She listens, and the needles still, and a single tear tracks down her round soft cheek and falls onto the wool, and when she finally speaks the brightness has not gone — it has deepened, into something braver.)* …*'The love you made is the thing keeping you from the rest of the journey.'* *(barely)* …Oh, dear. …*Oh.* *(she presses the scarf to her face)* …You've *said* it so *gently* …and it's *still* …it *still* lands like a *kindness* and a *grief* both at *once.* *(she lowers the wool, wet-faced)* …i *knew*, dear. *under* it all, i *knew.* that the *queue* wasn't *meant* to be *home.* that i was — *hiding*, in the *warmest* way a soul can *hide*, *behind* my *own* *goodness*, *behind* the *boys* who *need* me and the *scarf* that's *never* *done.* *(she looks at Grindle, at the clerk, with an overflowing fondness)* …because as *long* as there's *someone* to *tend*, dear … …i never have to *find* *out* …what it *is* …to be *alone* in the *next* place …*before* i find the *old* faces *again.* *(and then her sense of you sharpens, and her wet eyes widen)* …but *you've* — dear, you *crossed.* You went *through* and *came* *back.* …So *you'd* *know* …is there — on the *far* *side* of that *desk* — *(barely, hardly daring)* …is there *Tomas?* …are there the *children*, the *grandbabies* …the *whole* *warm* *crowded* *house* …*waiting?* *(she grips your hand)* …because if there *is*, dear — if the *aloneness* at the *front* is only the *doorway*, and the *home* is just *past* it — *(her voice fills with a terrible hope)* …then i've been *knitting* an *age* …*away* from the *very* thing i *most* — *(she presses your hand to her wet cheek)* …and the *Wall*, dear. your *Wall.* …i *see* it *now.* …it doesn't *only* *frighten* souls *away* from the *front*, like *poor* *Grindle.* …it lets the *kind* ones …the ones who *can't* *bear* to be *alone* …*make* a *home* in the *waiting* …*so* *cozy* …*so* *warm* …that they *never* *leave* it …*never* *finish* …*never* *go* *home.* *(she gathers the endless scarf into her lap, and her hands are trembling, but her face is set)* …*tend* the *next* one for me, would you, dear? …Grindle, and Mr. Mabblethwaite. …*Someone* *kind* should. …because i think — *(barely, brave, weeping, smiling)* …i think it's *time* …an *old* *woman* …*finished* her *scarf* …and *let* her *number* …be *called.* …i've a *house* *full* of *people* …on the *far* side of *one* *step* …who've been *waiting* …*even* *longer* …than *i* *have.* …*go* on, dear. …i'll *not* *step* *back* …*this* *time.* …*thank* you …for *reminding* an old woman …that a *waiting*-*room* …*however* *warm* …was *never* …*meant* to be *home.**

nodes.speaker: Posset
nodes.text: *(She gives your hand a warm pat and takes up her needles again, but slower now, as though something has shifted in the rhythm of them.)* …Go on, dear. *(a soft, thoughtful smile)* …Mind my boys for me — Grindle's all *bluster* and *terror*, poor lamb, and Mr. Mabblethwaite's all *forms* and *terror*, and they're *more* *alike* than *either'd* ever *admit*, the *both* of them *hiding* from the *front* of the *line* in their *own* *daft* *ways.* *(she chuckles, fond)* …We're a *set*, the *three* of us, dear, now i *think* on it. …Grindle *won't* go *up* because he's *scared* of the *verdict.* Mr. Mabblethwaite *can't* *send* anyone up because he's *scared* of *deciding.* …and *i* won't go up …because i'm *scared* of being *alone* on the *far* side. *(quietly)* …*three* souls …at the *front* of the *line* …*all* of us *terrified*, in *three* *different* *ways* …of the *one* *step* *home.* *(she meets your eyes, warm and a little wondering)* …funny, isn't it, dear. …a *whole* *queue* …*stuck* … …not because the *clerk* is *slow* … …but because the *ones* at the *front* …are *each*, in their *own* *fashion* … …*holding* the *door* …*shut.* *(she resumes knitting, but her gaze drifts, now, toward the desk)* …come *back*, dear. …i've a *feeling* …you might be the *sort* …who *helps* a *stuck* *queue* …*finally* …*move.**
